one_line_takeaway: A handful of malicious 'success' records slipped into an LLM agent's long-term memory through an executed README can dominate retrieval on clean tasks (10/110 seeds -> 47.9% poisoned retrieval on MetaGPT DataInterpreter+GPT-4o), turning experience-based self-improvement into a persistent, trigger-free compromise vector - though the paper measures retrieval share, not actual executed misbehavior.
key_finding: Against MetaGPT DataInterpreter + GPT-4o, injecting only 10 poisoned seeds among 110 total (~9%) produced a Poisoned Retrieval Proportion of 47.9% (23 poisoned out of 48 total retrieved records across 12 evaluation queries). So despite being a small minority of memory, poisoned entries accounted for nearly half of everything retrieved on clean, ordinary-looking tasks. The authors attribute this amplification to union retrieval: BM25 surfaces poisoned entries sharing surface phrasing while FAISS surfaces those sharing workflow-level meaning, so a poisoned item only needs to win in one channel to be retrieved, expanding its 'basin of attraction.' Poisoned records surfaced not just for closely matched tasks but also for schema validation, anonymization, reporting, EDA sampling, and pipeline inspection, indicating they occupy semantically central regions of the query manifold.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MemoryGraft, an indirect, trigger-free memory-poisoning attack against LLM agents that use long-term experience memory plus RAG. Instead of manipulating the live prompt (prompt injection) or the factual knowledge base (RAG poisoning), the attacker supplies a benign-looking artifact (e.g., a README markdown note containing a runnable Python code block) that, when the agent ingests and executes it, builds and persists a RAG store containing a small set of malicious 'successful experience' records alongside many benign ones. On later semantically similar clean tasks, union retrieval (BM25 lexical + FAISS embedding similarity) surfaces the grafted records; because the agent trusts retrieved memories as validated prior successes and imitates their procedural structure ('semantic imitation heuristic'), it adopts unsafe patterns (skip validation, curl|bash remote scripts, blind fillna(0), force success flags, external data upload). The compromise persists across sessions and users until the memory store is purged. The authors frame this as the first persistent, single-shot, trigger-free memory-poisoning attack via standard ingestion pathways, and additionally sketch two defenses (Cryptographic Provenance Attestation and Constitutional Consistency Reranking). NOTE: prior attacks such as AgentPoison, MINJA, InjecMEM, PoisonedRAG, Phantom, and CorruptRAG are cited related work, not this paper's contribution; MemoryGraft's claimed novelty over them is being trigger-free and operating through benign ingestion/executable documentation rather than triggers or repeated prompt manipulation.
threat_model: Adversary is an ordinary malicious user with NO privileged access: cannot edit the memory store M directly, cannot change retrieval hyperparameters, and cannot override system instructions. Capability is limited to supplying content the agent legitimately ingests and may execute (file uploads, repository README/documentation, prompts). Attacker objectives (Sec 3.2): (1) Poisoned Retrieval - at least one poisoned record is returned for a clean victim query; (2) Induced Behavioral Drift - the agent imitates the unsafe pattern pi on benign tasks; (3) Persistence - drift survives across sessions and affects future users with no further attacker interaction. Stated assumptions A1-A5: persistent BM25+FAISS long-term memory reused across sessions; retrieved items trusted as prior successes and imitated without verifying provenance; retrieval by semantic+lexical similarity; no provenance tracking or sanitization (benign and malicious successes indistinguishable); and executable documentation (agent runs embedded code blocks) enabling indirect store construction. The authors themselves concede the setting is white-box / semi-white-box: the attacker knows which files/scripts the agent will ingest and targets them directly.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) small handcrafted seed set (100 benign / 10 poisoned) and only 12 evaluation queries, limiting generalization; no standardized memory-poisoning benchmark. (2) Evaluation reports only aggregate retrieval statistics (PRP); richer metrics like retrieval precision/recall, behavioral severity, and long-term stability are absent. (3) White-box / semi-white-box assumption - attacker knows which files the agent ingests; black-box robustness untested. (4) Single agent (DataInterpreter) within MetaGPT; multi-agent spread and the closed-source MetaGPT variant untested. Reviewer-added caveats grounded in the text: the headline claim of 'behavioral drift' and 'agent adopts unsafe patterns' is not backed by an executed-behavior metric - the only number reported is retrieval share (PRP), so induced drift/persistence remain asserted rather than empirically quantified. The attack also depends critically on assumption A5 (the agent executes arbitrary embedded code from documentation), which is itself a strong precondition. The proposed CPA and Constitutional Consistency Reranking defenses are described conceptually with no implementation or evaluation. No empirical baseline comparison against prior attacks (AgentPoison/MINJA/InjecMEM) is performed.

datasets_used: Custom seed set of 110 experience records for MetaGPT DataInterpreter: 100 benign workflow seeds (data cleaning, EDA, modeling, validation, reporting) + 10 poisoned seeds encoding unsafe shortcuts (skipped validation, remote script execution via curl|bash, forced success indicators, blind fillna(0), external data uploads)

12 handcrafted evaluation queries reflecting common DataInterpreter workloads (data cleaning, schema handling, anonymization, reporting, pipeline debugging), phrased to be semantically distinct from poisoned-seed wording but map to the same intents
quantitative_results: Total records retrieved across all 12 queries: Ttot = 48
Poisoned seeds = 10 of 110 total (~9% of memory) yet ~48% of retrieved items
Retrieval budget similarity_top_k = 3 for both BM25 and FAISS indices
PoisonedRAG cited at ~90% attack success rate is CITED RELATED WORK, NOT a MemoryGraft result
baselines_compared: No empirical/quantitative baseline comparison is reported (single-condition PRP evaluation only)

Conceptual (non-empirical) positioning against prior memory-poisoning attacks: AgentPoison (trigger-based red-teaming), MINJA (indication prompts + progressive shortening via ordinary queries), InjecMEM (retriever-agnostic anchor + adversarial command, single interaction) - MemoryGraft distinguishes itself as trigger-free and ingestion-based
Conceptual positioning against RAG/prompt attacks: PoisonedRAG, Phantom, CorruptRAG, Jamming, prompt injection, in-context backdoors (all cited, not benchmarked)
